ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lemonade entry (FOODON_03301710); no specific mint lemonade entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Lemonade and similar carbonated soft drinks
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers lemonade-type carbonated beverages; no mint-specific variant code exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for mint lemonade beverage. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of mint lemonade is approximately 250 ml (250 g), as typically served in Singapore hawker centres and Indian restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Mint Lemonade — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (G00010686)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo record G00010686 for Mint Lemonade. All per-100g values preserved as reported. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB; set to -1 where not available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mint lemonade. GI estimated at 63 (medium) based on sugar-sweetened lemonade analogue from the University of Sydney GI Database (sucrose-sweetened soft drink, GI ~63). The beverage derives virtually all carbohydrate from sucrose (8.71g sugar / 9.02g total carbohydrate per 100g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (63) × carbohydrate per serving (22.55g per 250g serving) / 100 = 14.2, rounded to 14.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (8.71g/100g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage; sodium is low at 5mg/100g but sugar-driven insulin response promotes renal sodium retention and elevated blood pressure.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit mint lemonade in its standard sweetened form; requesting an unsweetened version with fresh mint and lemon juice eliminates the sugar-driven blood pressure risk while preserving the flavour profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.71g sugar per 100g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sucrose is absorbed rapidly, producing a sharp postprandial glucose spike with no glycaemic buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid standard mint lemonade due to its high free-sugar content and absence of fibre; an unsweetened version using stevia or erythritol as a substitute sweetener is a clinically acceptable alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased cardiovascular event risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; this beverage provides 93 kcal/100g almost entirely from sugar.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid sweetened mint lemonade and opt for the unsweetened variant, as eliminating the added sugar removes the primary cardiovascular risk factor in this beverage.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mint Lemonade (also known as Pudina Nimbu Pani) is a popular chilled beverage of Indian origin, widely served in Singapore's Indian restaurants and hawker centres. It is prepared by combining fresh mint leaves, lemon or lime juice, sugar, and chilled soda water, then blending or muddling the mint to release its aromatic oils. The drink is refreshing, tangy, and mildly sweet with a pronounced herbal coolness from the mint. Per 100g, it provides 93 kcal, almost entirely from sugar (8.71g), with negligible protein (0g) and fat (0g), and 9.02g total carbohydrate. Sodium is very low at 5mg/100g. The beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened drink and carries a medium glycemic index (~63) due to its sucrose content and absence of dietary fibre. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or hypertension in its standard sweetened form, as liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly and independently raises blood pressure risk. An unsweetened version using zero-calorie sweeteners is a suitable alternative. The drink is vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mint lemonade?
Is mint lemonade good for high blood pressure?
Is mint lemonade suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo canonical record G00010686 with confidence 0.95. All core macros and available micronutrients are taken from this approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using a sucrose-sweetened lemonade analogue, which is clearly documented.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero as this is a sugar-sweetened beverage with no protein-contributing ingredients. fat_g is naturally zero as the beverage contains no fat-bearing ingredients (mint, lemon juice, sugar, soda water). dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source. saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0 as the beverage contains no fat; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB for this beverage. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo record G00010686 for this beverage. GI of 63 is an analogous estimate based on sucrose-sweetened lemonade from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for mint lemonade was found in approved sources. GL per serving = 63 × (9.02 × 2.5) / 100 = 63 × 22.55 / 100 = 14.2 (reported as 14.3 after rounding). Cultural context: this beverage is popular in Indian cuisine and widely available in Singapore; multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) per G6 multilingual rule.
